title: AI Token Checks Before a Crypto Swap
keyQuestions: Can AI verify a token contract before a crypto swap?
Why can chatbots misread token risk?
Is the ticker enough to identify a token?
What should I avoid pasting into AI before checking a token?
Should I check the token before or after getting a quote?
How can I reduce wrong-network risk before swapping a token?
quickSteps: Identify the chain first, because the same ticker can appear on different networks and route support depends on the selected chain.
Verify the contract source for non-native tokens using official project references, trusted explorers, and the token selector in the live swap interface.
Compare route support before opening a timed quote, including input asset, network, destination asset, limits, and wallet compatibility.
Check wallet prompts and approvals carefully, especially if the token requires permission before a swap.
Stop before sending if the ticker, contract, network, route, or wallet facts do not match; refresh the quote only after the token identity is clear.

issueRouting.start: The user wants to use AI to understand a token before a crypto swap without relying on the chatbot as the final source of truth.

branches.if: The ticker looks familiar but the chain or contract is unclear
then: Identify the exact network where the token is held.
Check the contract source through official references and trusted explorers.
Avoid opening or sending to a timed quote until token identity is clear.

branches.if: The token is real but route support is uncertain
then: Compare the input asset, network, destination asset, limits, and wallet compatibility.
Use the live swap interface to confirm route support.
Do not rely on a chatbot explanation of the ticker alone.

branches.if: Funds have already been sent or the route state has changed
then: Stop asking general token questions and check the visible route status.
Confirm the source-chain transaction and route stage.
Share only narrow support details if escalation is needed.

riskNotes: A ticker is only a label; for non-native tokens, the contract address and network identify the asset.
AI may rely on old or incomplete information about migrations, contract upgrades, delistings, bridge changes, or route support.
A wallet can display a token even when the swap route does not support that exact asset or network.
Full screenshots can expose balances, wallet labels, browser extensions, partial addresses, route identifiers, and unrelated assets.
A quote timer should be used for execution after verification, not for researching token identity under pressure.
